claimed_contribution: The paper proposes and tests a novel downstream consequence of price promotions: incidental exposure can cause impatience in unrelated domains.
It advances a process account in which promotions act as reward cues that trigger reward seeking, which then increases impatience, and supports this with moderation and mediation evidence.
It provides a conceptual replication in a field setting to demonstrate external validity and managerial relevance.
context: Price promotions are treated as ubiquitous marketplace cues encountered across everyday retail and media touchpoints. Evidence comes from online participant pools, university student settings, and a university food court field environment.
design_and_data: Seven experiments (N = 1,795) compared incidental exposure to promotion cues versus control cues using poster evaluations, photographs of real-world signs/labels, and mock credit card offers. Samples included U.S.-based MTurk workers, college students, and food court visitors. The studies measured impatience using willingness to pay to avoid waiting, actual waiting time on loading screens/web pages, willingness to break a checkout rule to save time, and delay discounting in a consequential $100 gift-card task. Process tests examined moderation by reward sensitivity (BAS; Experiments 3a and 3b) and mediation by measured reward seeking (Experiment 4), with a field conceptual replication in Experiment 5.
focal_constructs: willingness to pay to avoid waiting
main_findings: Incidental exposure to price promotions increased impatience across multiple measures, including greater willingness to pay to avoid waiting, shorter actual wait times, greater willingness to break a rule to save time, and higher delay discounting.
The effect of promotion exposure on impatience was stronger for participants higher in reward sensitivity (BAS).
Reward seeking increased after promotion exposure and mediated the effect on impatience in a consequential intertemporal choice.
one_line_summary: Across seven experiments, incidental exposure to price promotions increased impatience in unrelated domains, partly because promotions heightened reward seeking.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior price-promotion research focused on immediate purchase benefits by linking incidental promotion exposure to out-of-domain impatience, and bridges it with reward-cue evidence showing that reward seeking can shift people toward present-oriented choices.
research_gap: Prior research has largely emphasized the immediate monetary and nonmonetary benefits of promotions and responses tied to purchase decisions. The paper identifies a gap on whether merely incidental exposure to promotions has downstream effects on unrelated behavior, specifically impatience.
research_question: Does incidental exposure to price promotions make consumers more impatient in unrelated contexts, even when they do not actually use a promotion? If so, is this effect explained by heightened reward seeking and conditioned by individual differences in reward sensitivity?
